comment: Brian (ElevenLabs) VO for the 300s Match 2 video. Story source: content/Stories/South-Korea-vs-Czech-Republic.md ('The Last Waltz of Sonaldo'). Lines are sized to fill each scene window; mux.mjs time-fits each line to its slot. `at` = start time in seconds on the global 300s timeline.

lines.text: Listen. Do you hear that? That is not just a crowd. That is thirty years of dreams... beating inside one man's chest.
Tonight, the greatest footballer Asia has ever produced begins his goodbye. And trust me... you are not ready for it.
Welcome back to WorldCup twenty-six Legends. Match Two of one hundred and four. South Korea versus the Czech Republic — Group A, under the lights of Guadalajara.
The Estadio Akron is shaking. Korean drums on one side. Czech songs on the other. Forty-eight thousand fans, and one question hanging in the warm Mexican night.
Can the Taegeuk Warriors give their captain the farewell he deserves?
Meet South Korea. Nine straight World Cups. Semi-finalists on home soil in two thousand two. And led by a man the whole world calls... Sonaldo.
Son Heung-min. Thirty-three years old. His fourth World Cup — and his last. Every touch, every sprint, every shot is now a goodbye written in lightning.
Behind him stands The Monster. Kim Min-jae — the wall that conquered Italy with Napoli. The defender that strikers see in their nightmares.
When the Monster locks the gate, entire nations are left waiting outside. But tonight, his cage faces its greatest escape artist.
And pulling the strings — Lee Kang-in. The magician from Paris who turns grass into a chessboard... and defenders into spectators.
Pace. Power. And poetry. That is the Korean storm rolling into Group A.
But storms have met Bohemia before... and broken against it. The Czech Republic. The heart of Europe. Built on patience, granite, and devastating precision.
Patrik Schick. The man who scored from the halfway line at a European Championship. He lives in the margins, finds space where none exists... and he has already calculated every possible outcome.
Tomas Soucek. The tower of Prague. Two metres of midfield iron, attacking every cross as if it owes him money.
And Adam Hlozek. The young wolf. Fast, fearless, and hungry to drag Czech football back into the light.
Patience against passion. Granite against lightning. THIS... is why we love the World Cup.
Because every great match hides a secret duel inside it. The Monster... against the Marksman. Kim Min-jae against Patrik Schick. Ninety minutes. One cage. One escape artist.
One of them will own this night. The other will dream about it for the rest of his life.
Our storytellers have looked into the night of June twelfth... and this is what they saw.
Forty-first minute. Son receives on the left. He cuts inside — the move that has beaten a thousand defenders — and he lets it fly.
GOAL! Top corner! Sonaldo! In the first dance of his farewell, the old magic makes the river of time stand still!
Look at these faces. Tears of pure joy. This is what football does — it turns grown men back into children.
But the Czechs rise. Schick drifts wide, escapes the Monster for the very first time all night... and the whole stadium forgets how to breathe.
And out of nowhere — Hwang Hee-chan! The tackle of a lifetime! The ball spins away... and with it, the Czech dream.
Full time. One, nil. A warrior's farewell begins with victory... and on the other side, a heartbreak that words cannot reach.
Three points for South Korea. A goal for the captain. And for the Czechs — two more chances to write a different ending.
That is the beauty of Group A. Nothing is decided... and everything is still possible.
Now here is where YOU come in. Did you see this coming? At WorldCup twenty-six dot world, you pick three of the forty-eight nations — and every single goal they score... scores for you.
Underdogs multiply your points. The leaderboard moves live with every goal. And it is completely free to play. Your three picks could change everything.

If this story gave you goosebumps — smash that like, share it with a football romantic, and subscribe. Because next, Canada opens its World Cup... and you do NOT want to miss what our storytellers have seen.
